dependencies.notes: Auth is completely non-functional without Redis and user-db. If either is down, all downstream services (orders, gateway) will also fail. Check dependencies first before assuming auth-service itself is broken.

common_issues.title: Users being logged out / sessions invalidated unexpectedly
symptoms: auth-service logs show 'token not found in cache'
likely_causes: Redis cluster restart or failover flushed in-memory sessions
JWT signing secret was rotated without overlap window
Redis eviction policy kicked out session keys under memory pressure
auth-service pod restarted and in-memory state was lost (shouldn't happen — check if Redis is being used correctly)

steps.action: Check Redis connectivity
steps.command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST ping
steps.if_fails: Redis is unreachable. See redis runbook RB-REDIS-001.

steps.action: Check Redis memory and eviction
steps.command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST info memory
steps.note: If used_memory is near maxmemory, eviction may be culprit. Check eviction policy with CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy.

steps.action: Check recent JWT secret rotation events
steps.command: kubectl get events -n production | grep secret
steps.note: Look for secret rotation in the last 24h. If rotation happened, verify both old and new keys are in JWKS endpoint.

steps.action: Verify JWKS endpoint returns both keys during rotation window
steps.command: curl -s https://auth.internal/auth/jwks | jq '.keys | length'
steps.expected: Should be >= 2 during rotation window, 1 otherwise
steps.note: If only 1 key is returned right after a rotation, tokens signed by old key will fail. This is the most common cause. Talk to whoever rotated the secret.

steps.action: If needed, manually force re-login for affected users
steps.command: N/A — there is no admin API for this yet. Users must log in again.
steps.note: TODO: add session invalidation endpoint. Known gap — see JIRA IAM-441.

common_issues.escalation: If issue persists after checking Redis and JWKS, escalate to #identity-team. Do not restart the auth-service pod without approval — it will invalidate more sessions.

common_issues.title: High 401 error rate (auth rejecting valid tokens)
symptoms: 401 spike visible in Datadog dashboard 'Auth Error Rate'
Logs contain 'JWT signature verification failed' or 'token expired'
Affects subset of users, not all
likely_causes: Secret rotation without overlap — see AUTH-001
Clock skew between auth-service and client (TOTP / token expiry edge cases)
Token TTL too short — check JWT_EXPIRY env var
Bug in upstream service not passing Authorization header correctly

steps.action: Sample failing tokens from logs and decode them
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/auth-service | grep 'signature' | tail -20
steps.note: Grab a sample JWT from logs and decode at jwt.io to check iat, exp, and kid fields. Do NOT share production tokens in Slack.

steps.action: Check auth-service clock drift
steps.command: kubectl exec -it deploy/auth-service -- date -u
steps.note: Compare with your local UTC time. More than 30 seconds drift is a problem, especially for TOTP.

steps.action: Check the kid field in failing tokens against active keys
steps.command: curl -s https://auth.internal/auth/jwks | jq '[.keys[].kid]'
steps.note: If the kid in the failing token doesn't match any key in JWKS, the signing key has been rotated and old tokens are dead.

steps.action: Check JWT_EXPIRY and JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRY env vars
steps.command: kubectl get deploy/auth-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env'
steps.note: Reasonable values: JWT_EXPIRY=900 (15 min), JWT_REFRESH_EXPIRY=86400 (24h). Very short values cause frequent logouts.

steps.action: Check if a specific upstream service is not forwarding auth headers
steps.command: Check gateway logs for requests with missing Authorization header. Usually this is a deploy misconfiguration.
steps.note: Sometimes the issue isn't auth at all — a service just forgot to pass the token through. Check with relevant team.

common_issues.escalation: If token kid mismatch is confirmed, this is a P1. Page the identity on-call immediately.

common_issues.title: Auth service pod OOMKilled / memory leak
symptoms: Pod restarts visible in kubectl get pods
OOMKilled exit code in pod describe
Memory grows steadily over several hours then crashes
likely_causes: HTTP client connection pool not being closed after user-db or Redis calls
Large session payloads being cached in-process as well as in Redis
Known issue: see JIRA IAM-388 (not yet fixed as of this writing)

steps.action: Confirm OOMKill
steps.command: kubectl describe pod -l app=auth-service | grep -A5 'Last State'

steps.action: Check memory limit and current usage
steps.command: kubectl top pods -l app=auth-service
steps.note: Current memory limit is 512Mi. If consistently above 400Mi, consider bumping to 768Mi as a short-term workaround. File ticket for proper investigation.

steps.action: Take heap dump before restarting (if pod is still running)
steps.command: kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- python -c "import tracemalloc; tracemalloc.start()" 2>/dev/null || echo 'Use py-spy or attach debugger'
steps.note: Honestly this is hard to do in production. If you can, get a heap snapshot. Otherwise just bump the memory limit and file a proper bug.

steps.action: Temporarily increase memory limit to stabilise
steps.command: kubectl set resources deployment/auth-service --limits=memory=768Mi
steps.note: This buys time. Do NOT do this repeatedly without investigating root cause.

steps.action: Check connection pool configuration
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/auth-service -- env | grep -i pool
steps.note: Look for DB_POOL_SIZE and REDIS_POOL_SIZE. These should not be unbounded. If they are, set them. See config docs — or ask Alice.

common_issues.escalation: If pod is restarting more than once per hour, page the identity on-call. Auth instability cascades to all other services.

common_issues.title: Account lockout not working / brute force attempts
symptoms: Spike in /auth/login requests from few IPs
Lockout counter not incrementing despite repeated failures
likely_causes: Lockout state stored per-pod in-memory instead of Redis (known bug, may or may not be fixed in current version)
Rate limiting configured on global IP rather than per-user

steps.action: Check if lockout is hitting Redis or in-memory
steps.command: kubectl exec deploy/auth-service -- redis-cli -h $REDIS_HOST keys 'lockout:*' | head -20
steps.note: If you see lockout keys in Redis, locking is working. If empty during an active brute force, it's in-memory (broken).

steps.action: Block attacking IPs at WAF level immediately
steps.command: Log into Cloudflare dashboard > Security > WAF > Custom Rules > Add IP block rule
steps.note: This is the fastest mitigation. Get the attacking IPs from gateway logs: kubectl logs deploy/gateway | grep 'POST /auth/login' | awk '{print $1}' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head

steps.action: Temporarily tighten rate limit on login endpoint
steps.command: Update gateway rate limit config for /auth/login — set to 5 req/min per IP. See gateway runbook for how to do this.
steps.note: Be careful not to lock out legitimate users. Coordinate with customer support.

steps.action: Check if AUTH_LOCKOUT_BACKEND env var is set to 'redis'
steps.command: kubectl get deploy/auth-service -o json | jq '.spec.template.spec.containers[0].env[] | select(.name=="AUTH_LOCKOUT_BACKEND")'
steps.note: If missing or set to 'memory', this is the bug. Add the env var and redeploy. Check with Alice first.

common_issues.escalation: Active brute force against production is a security incident. Notify security-team@example.com and page identity on-call.

common_issues.title: TOTP / MFA codes being rejected
symptoms: Users with MFA enabled cannot log in
Logs show 'TOTP validation failed' for codes that should be valid
Only affects MFA users, regular password login works
likely_causes: Auth-service host clock drift — NTP sync issue (most common)
VM was suspended and resumed without re-syncing clock

steps.action: Check clock drift on auth-service pods
steps.command: for pod in $(kubectl get pods -l app=auth-service -o name); do echo $pod; kubectl exec $pod -- date -u; done
steps.note: Compare each pod time against actual UTC. TOTP has a 30s window (1 step). Drift over 30s will reject valid codes. Drift over 90s will reject even with generous window.

steps.action: Force NTP sync if drifted
steps.command: kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- chronyc -a makestep
steps.note: This only fixes the running pod. If it's a VM image issue, the drift will return after next pod restart. File infra ticket.

steps.action: Temporarily widen TOTP tolerance window
steps.command: kubectl set env deployment/auth-service TOTP_STEP_WINDOW=2
steps.note: Default is 1 (±30s). Setting to 2 gives ±60s. Setting above 3 is not recommended for security reasons. This is a temporary fix.

steps.action: Verify fix by checking recent TOTP success rate
steps.command: kubectl logs deploy/auth-service | grep 'TOTP' | grep -c 'success\|fail'
steps.note: Ratio should flip back to mostly successes. If not, the issue is something else — possibly a bug in the TOTP seed storage.

common_issues.escalation: If NTP sync doesn't resolve it and drift isn't the issue, escalate to #identity-team. Don't guess at TOTP seed issues.

general_health_checks: kubectl get pods -l app=auth-service — all pods should be Running
Check Datadog dashboard 'Auth Service Overview' for error rate, p99 latency, and pod restarts
kubectl logs deploy/auth-service --since=15m | grep -i error — should be near zero outside incidents
known_gaps: No admin API to manually invalidate sessions — users must re-login (JIRA IAM-441)
Lockout state may be per-pod if AUTH_LOCKOUT_BACKEND is not explicitly set — verify on each deploy
Heap dump procedure in prod is not well defined — see JIRA IAM-388
GDPR deletion job does not remove auth audit logs — compliance gap, escalate to Alice
notes: Auth is a shared critical dependency. Any change to auth-service in production must be coordinated with the gateway and orders teams. Do not rotate JWT secrets without notifying all teams first. There is a runbook for secret rotation specifically — find it on Confluence (search 'JWT rotation runbook' — it was last updated a while ago so some steps may be stale).
